claim: XBOW autonomous hacking agent became #1 ranked hacker on HackerOne in August 2025 using only black-box access, no source code
actionable: Evaluate agent capabilities on realistic security tasks like bug bounty platforms to ground capability claims in external verification
source_title: Inside the Rise of Autonomous AI Hackers: XBOW's Oege de Moor

claim: XBOW found a remote code execution vulnerability in Bing Image Search for $3,000 bounty using reconnaissance and systematic endpoint testing
actionable: Use autonomous agents for security testing; reconnaissance followed by systematic hypothesis testing can find complex vulnerabilities
source_title: Inside the Rise of Autonomous AI Hackers: XBOW's Oege de Moor

claim: Model alloys (alternating between multiple models like Sonnet 4 and Gemini 2.5 at each step) outperform either model alone for hacking
actionable: Use model alloys for complex tasks: flip a coin at each step to choose between different models; they compensate for each other's weaknesses
source_title: Inside the Rise of Autonomous AI Hackers: XBOW's Oege de Moor

claim: CVE publication-to-exploitation lag has gone negative: most CVEs are exploited before publication, not after, demanding faster defensive AI
actionable: Recognize that vulnerability windows have compressed; deploy defensive AI agents to find vulnerabilities faster than adversaries can exploit them
source_title: Inside the Rise of Autonomous AI Hackers: XBOW's Oege de Moor

claim: Within 6-9 months, open-weight models will reach autonomous hacking capability of frontier models, requiring immediate defensive deployment
actionable: Prioritize agent-based security defense now; within 9 months, adversaries will have equivalent capabilities via open-weight models
source_title: Inside the Rise of Autonomous AI Hackers: XBOW's Oege de Moor
